structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: The preface of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document provides an overview and historical context for the specification. It introduces key elements such as the document's identification number (658), its status (published) and part of the AUTOSAR Classic Platform standard release R25-11. The section details a comprehensive change history, highlighting modifications in error codes, transformer parameters, and API definitions over multiple releases. Importantly, it outlines the document’s structure and references related standards, ensuring clarity on its applicability to automotive applications only.

structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document introduces the fundamental concepts and architecture of transformers in AUTOSAR systems. It defines transformers as mechanisms that enable data transformation for linearization and modification, often used within transformer chains executed by the Real-Time Environment (RTE) for intra-ECU and inter-ECU communication. Key components include well-defined function signatures per communication relation, which dictate how data is transformed based on the transmitted elements. Transformers can be chained together, with each subsequent transformer using the output of the previous one as input, allowing for flexible addition of functionalities such as safety or security protection to a serialized stream.

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the acronyms and abbreviations pertinent to the text. It specifies that no additional acronyms or abbreviations are introduced in this particular document beyond those already defined in the AUTOSAR glossary, indicating a reliance on an external source for standard terminology. While there are no specific tables provided within this section, it emphasizes the importance of adhering to established definitions and terms as outlined by AUTOSAR, ensuring consistency across technical documents and communications related to transformer specifications.

structure.title: Related documentation
structure.summary: The "Related Documentation" section in the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines additional resources and references that are pertinent to understanding the specifications and implementation details of transformers. It introduces key concepts such as transformer architectures, their components like self-attention mechanisms and feed-forward networks, and highlights important relationships between these elements and other related technologies. The section also describes constraints and processes involved in designing and optimizing transformer models, including considerations for training efficiency and model performance. While no specific tables are mentioned, the summary provides a comprehensive overview of the supplementary information available to users seeking deeper insights into transformers.

nodes.title: Input documents
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the input documents required for transformer development in an AUTOSAR (Automotive Open System Architecture) context. Key concepts include the Glossary, General Requirements on Basic Software Modules, and various templates such as the System Template and Software Component Template. These documents provide essential definitions, constraints, and structures necessary for creating compliant software components. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, these input documents form a foundational framework that ensures consistency and adherence to AUTOSAR standards throughout the development process.

structure.title: Constraints and assumptions
structure.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" titled "Constraints and Assumptions" outlines the limitations and underlying assumptions that guide the design and implementation of transformer models. It introduces key concepts such as model architecture constraints, data preprocessing requirements, and computational resource limitations. The document also describes important relationships between these constraints and the performance of the transformer, highlighting how certain architectural choices can impact efficiency and accuracy. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, the section emphasizes the need for careful consideration of these factors to ensure optimal model performance under practical conditions.

nodes.title: Limitations
nodes.summary: This section discusses the limitations of data transformation and communication in the context of transformers as specified in "General Specification of Transformers.pdf." It introduces key concepts such as VariableDataPrototypes, ClientServerOperations, TriggerInterfaces, and ISignals. The document outlines that transformations are most efficient for large complex data elements over busses with large PDU sizes, while small PDUs can lead to inefficiencies due to the need to span byte arrays across multiple messages. Importantly, it restricts the transformation of whole PDUs within the RTE, as these do not exist in this context, and instead allows for independent transformations of individual data elements that can be aggregated into larger PDUs. The maximum length of transformer chains is set to 255 due to memory constraints, reflecting current use cases.

nodes.title: Applicability to car domains
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the applicability of transformer models in automotive domains. It highlights that there are no specific restrictions when applying transformers to car-related tasks, indicating a broad scope for their use. While the content does not introduce new key concepts or definitions, it suggests that transformers can be effectively utilized across various aspects of the automotive industry without limitations. The section likely implies that the technical details and specifications provided in earlier sections are generally applicable to automotive applications, though no specific tables with detailed technical data are mentioned in this particular excerpt.

structure.title: Dependencies to other modules
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document discusses the dependencies between the transformer module and other AUTOSAR Software Components (SWCs). It explicitly states that there are no dependencies on any AUTOSAR SWS modules, indicating a modular design where the transformer operates independently. This independence suggests that the transformer can function without relying on specific SWCs provided by other components of the system, which is crucial for maintaining flexibility and reducing inter-module coupling. While no tables or additional technical details are mentioned in this excerpt, it sets the stage for understanding how the transformer module interacts (or does not interact) with other parts of the system.

nodes.title: File structure
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the file structure, detailing how various components and data are organized within transformer models. It introduces key concepts such as model parameters, configuration files, and checkpoint directories, which are essential for understanding the overall architecture. The summary outlines the hierarchical relationship between these elements, emphasizing that each component plays a specific role in the operation of the transformer model. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, it is implied that detailed specifications regarding file organization and directory structures are provided to ensure proper implementation and maintenance of the models.

nodes.title: Code file structure
nodes.summary: This section outlines the code file structure for transformers, defining them as BSW (Software and System Workmanship) modules according to [2, SWS BSW General]. It highlights that while this is a general guideline, specific deviations are documented in the corresponding SWS documents for individual transformers. This structure helps ensure consistency across transformer implementations while allowing flexibility where necessary.

nodes.title: Header file structure
nodes.summary: This section outlines the header file structure for transformers, which are categorized as BSW (Software and System Workbench) modules according to [2, SWS BSW General]. Key components include the standardized structure of transformer headers and specific deviations outlined in the corresponding SWS documents. The relationship between general specifications and module-specific variations is highlighted, emphasizing that while a unified framework exists, individual transformers may have unique implementations or exceptions.

structure.title: Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document focuses on tracing requirements for transformers and their modules. It outlines how specific SRS (System Requirements Specification) requirements are satisfied by corresponding SW (Software) specifications, such as naming conventions, error handling, and data processing capabilities. Key concepts include transformer classes, abstract errors, and version information functions. The section emphasizes the relationships between different software components and their roles in ensuring robust and flexible transformer operations, including support for concurrent execution and error management.

structure.title: Functional Specification
structure.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the functional requirements and specifications for transformers in an AUTOSAR system. Key concepts include stateful vs. stateless transformers, transformer chains, and the importance of defining output formats, endianness, and data semantics. The document emphasizes that transformers must handle unexpected input data gracefully, support re-entrancy to accommodate concurrent execution, and optimize execution where possible by sharing results from initial transformations across multiple signal paths. These specifications ensure consistent and efficient data transformation between ECUs while maintaining compatibility with different architectures.

nodes.title: Buffer Handling
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the buffer handling mechanisms for transformers. It introduces two modes: in-place and out-of-place buffering, each with specific requirements and constraints. Key concepts include the use of input and output buffers, with the transformer either modifying or not altering the original data based on the chosen mode. The section also details how buffer sizes are calculated by Rte and specifies that certain transformers must use out-of-place buffering due to restrictions in modifying original data within the SWC context. Important relationships and constraints include the first and last transformers in a chain always using out-of-place buffering, while intermediate transformers may use either mode based on their position in the chain.

nodes.title: Transformer Classes
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document discusses the categorization and usage of transformers in different classes based on their functionality. It introduces key transformer classes such as Serializer, Safety, Security, and Custom, with a constraint that only one transformer per class can be used in each transformer chain. The document also notes that additional transformer classes may be defined in future AUTOSAR releases, highlighting the evolving nature of these specifications.

nodes.title: Serializer
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the Serializer transformer, detailing its role in converting complex data elements (or no data for triggers) into a byte array for transmission. Key concepts include the serialization process and support for specific types of variable-size arrays. The serializer must adhere to defined upstream requirements and handle all possible complex data inputs. Importantly, it does not support "old-world" variable-size array data types but works with "new-world" ones, as detailed in referenced documents.

nodes.title: Safety
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document focuses on the safety aspects of transformers used in communication systems. It introduces key concepts such as a safety transformer's role in protecting against unintentional modifications to ensure safe data transmission between Electronic Control Units (ECUs). The document outlines specific requirements for the safety transformer, including ensuring the correct order and content of data transmissions, which can be achieved through mechanisms like sequence counters and checksums. These components are directly linked to upstream requirements defined in SRS_Xfrm_00001, emphasizing their importance in meeting overall system safety standards.

nodes.title: Security
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document focuses on the security aspects of transformers used in communication between Electronic Control Units (ECUs) to ensure secure data transmission. Key concepts include protecting inter-ECU communications, ensuring data authenticity, integrity, and freshness. The security transformer fulfills these requirements by implementing mechanisms such as sequence counters and checksums. These components are directly linked to specific upstream requirements, emphasizing the importance of robust security measures in maintaining the reliability and safety of bus communication systems.

nodes.title: Custom
nodes.summary: This section discusses custom transformers in the context of the AUTOSAR framework. It explains that while standard transformers are predefined, custom transformers can be developed by any party involved in the development process to address specific needs not covered by existing standards. These custom transformers can be documented as Component Description Documents (CDDs). The relationship between these custom components and the broader AUTOSAR architecture is highlighted, emphasizing flexibility and adaptability within the framework.

nodes.title: Error Handling
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the error handling mechanisms for transformers in a system where errors are communicated to the Real-Time Execution (RTE) layer. Key concepts include the distinction between soft and hard errors, with specific return code ranges assigned to each type. The RTE decides whether to continue or abort the transformer chain based on these error codes. Importantly, if a transformer encounters a situation that cannot produce valid output, it must signal this as a hard error, leaving the output buffer unchanged. This section also defines unique error sets for each transformer class and enforces that transformers can only return errors relevant to their class. The summary notes that soft errors in early stages of the chain might be masked by subsequent hard errors, highlighting potential issues with state transitions in dependent components like state machines.

nodes.title: Errors of Serializer Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on detailing errors associated with serializer transformers. It introduces key error codes and their classifications, including soft and hard errors, which are crucial for understanding the failure modes during data serialization processes. The summary outlines a table that defines various error conditions such as successful serialization, lack of data, payload length exceeding expectations, and mismatches in protocol or interface versions. These definitions help in identifying specific issues that can arise during the serialization process and guide appropriate error handling mechanisms.

nodes.title: Errors of Safety Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document focuses on errors associated with safety transformers. It introduces error codes and their corresponding descriptions to help identify issues in communication and data validation. Key concepts include different types of errors such as soft and hard, categorized by whether they affect the validity or availability of data. The relationships between various error conditions are detailed through specific error names and codes, highlighting scenarios where repeated counters, counter jumps, check failures, and lack of new data impact safety transformer operations. Technical details from tables provide a comprehensive list of errors, their codes, types, and descriptions, ensuring clear understanding and proper handling of potential issues in the system.

nodes.title: Errors of Security Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the errors associated with security transformers. It introduces key error codes and their corresponding descriptions, which are essential for understanding the security status of data communication. Specifically, it defines three error types: `E_OK`, indicating secure communication; `E_SEC_NOT_AUTH`, signifying a failure in data authentication; and `E_SEC_NOT_FRESH`, denoting that the data is not up-to-date. These errors are categorized as "soft" issues, implying they do not necessarily disrupt the primary function of the transformer but indicate potential security vulnerabilities that need to be addressed. The table provides clear mappings between error codes and their descriptions, facilitating easier identification and handling of these security-related issues in practical applications.

nodes.title: Errors of Custom Transformers
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the errors associated with custom transformers. It introduces an error coding system to categorize and identify different types of errors that can occur during the operation of these transformers. The key components include error codes ranging from 0x00 to 0xFF, where specific soft (0x01-0x7F) and hard (0x80-0xFF) errors are defined based on their severity and nature. Additionally, it outlines that an "E_OK" status with a code of 0x00 indicates no error has occurred, providing a clear baseline for error detection and handling in custom transformer systems.

nodes.title: Error Classification
nodes.summary: The section "Error Classification" in the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on categorizing errors that can occur during the operation and maintenance of transformers. It introduces key concepts such as primary and secondary side errors, which are further classified into transient and permanent faults. The section outlines important relationships between different types of errors and their potential impacts on transformer performance. Although no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it emphasizes the need for detailed error tracking and management to ensure reliable operation.

nodes.title: Development Errors
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document focuses on defining development errors within the transformer module. It introduces key error codes and their corresponding values that are used to identify specific types of errors during the development process. The document outlines four main categories of errors, including calling an API service before initialization or after deinitialization, selecting an invalid configuration set, passing incorrect parameters, and using invalid pointers. These error codes are prefixed with MIP (Module Implementation Prefix) as defined in [SWS_BSW_00102], ensuring consistency across the module's implementation.

nodes.title: Runtime Errors
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" titled "Runtime Errors" (Section 7.4.2) discusses the absence of runtime errors in the context of transformer models. It indicates that during the execution phase, no unexpected or unhandled exceptions occur, ensuring smooth operation without interruptions due to runtime issues. While there are no specific tables provided within this section, it implies a robust error handling mechanism is not necessary for the described implementation, suggesting an efficient and reliable design.

nodes.title: Production Errors
nodes.summary: The section "7.4.3 Production Errors" in the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" asserts that there are no production errors identified within the context discussed. This statement implies a high level of quality control and adherence to manufacturing standards during the transformer production process. While this section does not introduce any new key concepts, definitions, or components, it underscores the absence of defects or issues in the production phase, which is crucial for ensuring the reliability and performance of the transformers. The summary reflects that the document maintains a focus on quality assurance throughout its specifications without detailing specific technical metrics or processes related to error detection or correction mechanisms.

nodes.title: Extended Production Errors
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on Extended Production Errors for transformers. It outlines specific types of errors that can occur during the manufacturing process and provides detailed specifications to address these issues. Key relationships are established between error types and their potential impacts on transformer performance, while constraints related to acceptable error levels are defined. Although no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, the section likely includes technical details such as error thresholds, detection methods, and corrective actions to ensure quality control throughout production.

nodes.title: XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the error condition `XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE`, which occurs when a transformer fails to produce output due to malformed input data. Key concepts include the detection criteria for this error, where it is triggered by non-conforming input formats and not by any secondary parameters or time constraints. The document specifies that this error must be implemented in every transformer where such an issue could arise, as mandated by upstream requirements SRS_BSW_00466 and SRS_BSW_00469. This ensures consistent handling of malformed messages across all transformers, enhancing system reliability and robustness.

nodes.title: Error Notification
nodes.summary: The section "Error Notification" in the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" (Section 7.5) outlines the mechanisms and protocols for error reporting within transformer systems as defined in [2, SWS BSW General]. It introduces key concepts such as error codes, notification methods, and the hierarchical structure of error handling. The section describes how errors are categorized and reported to ensure timely and effective system diagnostics. While no specific tables are mentioned, it emphasizes the importance of consistent error reporting protocols across different components of the transformer system to maintain operational reliability.

structure.title: API specification
structure.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the API specification for transformers, detailing how to interact with transformer models through application programming interfaces. It introduces key concepts such as API endpoints and request/response formats, which are essential for developers integrating transformer models into their applications. The summary outlines important relationships between different API components and any constraints or processes that must be followed when making API calls. While specific technical details from tables are not provided in the given content, this section likely includes examples of valid requests and responses, as well as error handling mechanisms to ensure robust integration.

nodes.title: Imported types
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the requirements for imported data types in transformer modules. It specifies that transformers must use certain types defined by RTE in their Module Interlink Types Header file and further defines specific imported types from various header files, such as `Rte.h` and `Std_Types.h`. The key components include `Rte_Cs_TransactionHandleType`, `Std_ExtractProtocolHeaderFieldsType`, among others. These definitions are constrained by upstream requirements, particularly SRS_Xfrm_00002, ensuring consistency across the system.

nodes.title: Type definitions
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the definition and characteristics of the `{Mip}_ConfigType` data structure. It is defined as a structure type that contains initialization data for transformers, with specific implementation details left to be determined by the developers. The type is referenced in header files designated for the Mip module, indicating its availability and usage within the system. This definition is closely tied to upstream requirements SRS_BSW_00404 and SRS_BSW_00441, ensuring that it aligns with broader system specifications.

nodes.title: Function definitions
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the generic interfaces for transformers and provides specific naming conventions for transformer APIs. It introduces key concepts such as `transformerId`, which is derived based on the configuration parameters like `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef`. The document details how the name pattern for `transformerId` varies depending on whether `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef` exists and, if so, the components involved (such as `ComponentName`, `p`, and `o`). Additionally, it explains that multiple transformer functions can be generated from a single `XfrmSignal` when an ISignal or ISignalGroup is referenced by several `XfrmImplementationMappings`.

nodes.title: <Mip>_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields
nodes.summary: This section describes the API function `<Mip>_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields`, which extracts relevant protocol header fields and message result types from a buffer. Key concepts include the function's parameters (input buffer, buffer length) and output arguments (message type and message result). The function is synchronous and reentrant, returning `E_OK` on success or `E_NOT_OK` on error. It is constrained to handle message types used for client-server communication (REQUEST, RESPONSE, OK, ERROR), as defined by the upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00002.

nodes.title: <Mip>_<transformerId>
nodes.summary: This section of the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the API functions for transformers used in various communication contexts such as Sender/Receiver and Client/Server communications. Key concepts include the `Mip` (Module Implementation Prefix) and `transformerId`, which are used to define unique function names for these transformations. The section describes the parameters, return values, and behavior of these functions, emphasizing their role in transforming data during runtime. Important relationships are established between these transformer functions and specific communication roles, such as being the first or non-first transformers in a chain. Constraints include the handling of errors, especially hard errors, and the conditions under which certain parameters may be ignored. Technical details specify the service names, parameter types, buffer management, and return values for each function, providing a comprehensive guide on their implementation and usage.

nodes.title: <Mip>_Inv_<transformerId>
nodes.summary: This section of the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" describes the API function `<Mip>_Inv_<transformerId>` for various communication scenarios including Sender/Receiver, Client/Server, and external trigger events. Key concepts include the definition and parameters of this function, its synchronization properties, reentrancy, and return values. The section also outlines specific rules for deriving variable prototype parts based on transformer roles within a transformation chain. Important relationships are established between this function and other components such as ISignals, SystemSignals, and TransformationTechnologies, with constraints on when and how the function should be implemented depending on its position in the chain. Technical details include parameters like `buffer`, `bufferLength`, and optional transaction handles for synchronization and data handling.

nodes.title: <Mip>_Init
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the API function `<Mip>_Init`, which initializes a transformer for further processing. Key concepts include the function's synchronous nature and non-reentrant property, as well as its parameter `config` that points to configuration data. The initialization process is constrained by specific upstream requirements (SRS_BSW_00407, SRS_BSW_00411) and follows rules defined in [SWS_BSW_001021] for variable prototype derivation. The function does not return any value or have output parameters, making it a void function that is declared in the `<Mip>.h` header file.

nodes.title: <Mip> _DeInit
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the definition and implementation details of the API function `<Mip>_DeInit`, which is responsible for deinitializing a transformer. Key concepts include its synchronous, non-reentrant nature with no parameters or return values. The function's availability is specified in the header file `<Mip>.h`. Additionally, it references upstream requirements such as SRS_BSW_00407 and SRS_Xfrm_00002, establishing a connection between this function and broader system specifications. The section also introduces the term "Module Implementation Prefix" (Mip) as defined in [SWS_BSW_00102], which is crucial for understanding how to implement specific transformer modules.

nodes.title: <Mip>_GetVersionInfo
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document describes the API function `<Mip>_GetVersionInfo`, which retrieves version information for a transformer module. Key concepts include the service name and its syntax, with parameters and return values specified. The function is synchronous and reentrant, returning the version information to a provided pointer. Importantly, it references upstream requirements such as SRS_BSW_00407 and SRS_Xfrm_00002, linking this function's implementation to broader system specifications. The module implementation prefix (Mip) is also defined here, crucial for identifying the specific transformer module whose version information is being requested.

nodes.title: Callback notifications
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" discusses the absence of callback notifications within the transformer framework. It indicates that there are no mechanisms for invoking callbacks at specific points during the operation of a transformer model. This implies that developers or users must implement their own event handling or rely on alternative methods to trigger actions based on certain conditions, as the system itself does not provide built-in notification functions. While this section does not introduce any new key concepts or components, it sets an expectation for how external interactions with the transformer should be managed, highlighting a constraint in the design that may impact user experience and integration strategies.

nodes.title: Scheduled functions
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" discusses the absence of universal scheduled functions for transformers. It highlights that there are no standardized scheduled operations applicable to all transformer models, indicating a lack of uniformity in maintenance practices across different transformer types. The summary does not introduce any specific key concepts or definitions but emphasizes the variability in maintenance schedules among transformers. No technical details from tables are mentioned as they are not present in this particular section.

nodes.title: Expected interfaces
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" titled "Expected interfaces" discusses the absence of any anticipated external interfaces for transformers. It indicates that there are no predefined or required interfaces that need to be implemented or adhered to, which is a critical aspect in the design and integration of transformer components within larger systems. While this section does not introduce specific technical details or relationships between different components, it sets an expectation that developers or engineers should focus on internal functionalities rather than external interactions when working with transformers.

structure.title: Sequence diagrams
structure.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" discusses the topic of sequence diagrams but notes that none are included in the document. The absence of sequence diagrams implies that no specific interactions or workflows between components are visually represented through this diagrammatic notation, which is typically used to illustrate the sequence of messages exchanged between objects over time. While there are no technical details from tables provided in this section, it highlights a gap in the documentation regarding the procedural aspects of the system's operation and interaction sequences.

structure.title: Configuration specification
structure.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the configuration specification for transformers, detailing how they are configured at both system and software component levels. It introduces key concepts such as containers and parameters, providing a template for parameter specification in Chapter 10.1 to ensure clarity. The document explains that configurations from these levels can be used to generate an EcuC (Electronic Control Unit Configuration) of the transformer. Additionally, it specifies the structure and parameters of transformers in Section 10.2, though the exact details are not provided here.

nodes.title: How to read this chapter
nodes.summary: This section provides guidance on how to interpret and understand the content of Chapter 10.1 in "General Specification of Transformers.pdf," which focuses on introducing configuration specifications. It emphasizes that readers should refer to a specific external source for detailed information, highlighting the importance of external documentation in comprehending the chapter's technical details. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, this section sets the stage for understanding the foundational concepts necessary for reading subsequent sections on transformer configurations.

nodes.title: Containers and configuration parameters
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" outlines the configuration parameters for transformers in an AUTOSAR context. It introduces key components such as `EcucModuleDef Xfrm`, which defines the module and its general configuration parameters, and `XfrmImplementationMapping`, used to map transformers to specific BswModuleEntries. The section emphasizes that these configurations are automatically generated based on DataTransformationSet information, ensuring consistency across different transformer implementations. Importantly, it specifies that post-build variant support is not enabled for this module, limiting the flexibility of configuration variants to a pre-compile state.

nodes.title: XfrmGeneral
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the `XfrmGeneral` container, which houses general configuration parameters for a module. Key concepts include `XfrmDevErrorDetect`, `XfrmInstanceId`, and `XfrmVersionInfoApi`, each with specific roles such as enabling development error detection, specifying the instance ID, and activating or deactivating version information API respectively. The document outlines that these parameters are included within the `XfrmGeneral` container under the parent `Xfrm`. Importantly, it specifies default values, multiplicity constraints, and configuration classes for each parameter, emphasizing their pre-compile time nature and lack of variant-specific configurations.

nodes.title: XfrmImplementationMapping
nodes.summary: This section of the "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" document outlines the `XfrmImplementationMapping` container and its key parameters for defining how transformers are implemented in a system. It introduces concepts such as `XfrmTransformerClassExtractProtocolHeaderFields`, `XfrmCSTransactionHandleImplementationDataTypeRef`, and `XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef`, which specify details about the transformer's implementation, including handling of protocol headers, transaction handles, and variable data prototypes. The document also describes constraints on using these parameters, particularly in scenarios involving intra-ECU communication and end-to-end (E2E) protected communication, ensuring that each receiver has its own state machine to avoid conflicts during data access.

nodes.title: XfrmSignal
nodes.summary: This section of "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the `XfrmSignal` container and its related configurations in the context of transforming signals for system descriptions. Key concepts include the definition of `XfrmSignal`, which references a signal that transports transformed data, and the choice between an individual signal (`ISignal`) or a group of signals (`ISignalGroup`). The document details the multiplicity constraints (0..1) and dependencies, including nested containers like `XfrmSignalChoice` for selecting between ISignal or ISignalGroup references. Importantly, it outlines how these choices are configured through specific parameters such as `XfrmISignalRef` and `XfrmISignalGroupRef`, each with their own foreign reference types and constraints, ensuring a flexible yet structured approach to signal transformation in system descriptions.

nodes.title: XfrmDemEventParameterRefs
nodes.summary: This section of the document "General Specification of Transformers.pdf" focuses on the `XfrmDemEventParameterRefs` container and related meta-classes in the context of AUTOSAR. Key concepts include the definition and configuration parameters for error handling, specifically referencing `DemEventParameter` elements that are invoked via the API `Dem_SetEventStatus`. The section introduces important relationships between different components such as `EcucParamConfContainerDef`, `XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE`, and `SystemSignal`. It also details the multiplicity, configuration parameters, and post-build variant configurations for these references. Relevant technical details from tables include attributes like `SwcInternalBehavior` and `SymbolProps`, which are crucial for understanding how error statuses are managed and mapped within the system.
